quiz_questions.question_text: If a mid-ocean ridge has symmetric magnetic stripes and the stripe 50 km from the ridge axis is 5 million years old, what is the full spreading rate?
quiz_questions.explanation: The half-spreading rate is 50 km / 5 million years = 10 km/Myr = 1 cm/year. Since spreading is symmetric, the full spreading rate (both plates moving apart) is 2 × 1 cm/year = 2 cm/year. The full rate measures the total separation between the two plates.

quiz_questions.question_text: A magnetic anomaly pattern shows that ocean floor 200 km east of a ridge axis is 10 million years old. What is the half-spreading rate on the eastern side?
quiz_questions.explanation: Half-spreading rate = distance / time = 200 km / 10 million years = 20 km/Myr. Converting: 20 km = 2,000,000 cm, so 2,000,000 cm / 1,000,000 years = 2 cm/year. This measures how fast the eastern plate moves away from the ridge.

quiz_questions.question_text: GPS stations on two plates show they are moving apart at 6 cm/year. How far apart will they be in 1 million years?
quiz_questions.explanation: Distance = rate × time = 6 cm/year × 1,000,000 years = 6,000,000 cm = 60,000 m = 60 km. GPS measurements provide the most direct and precise modern measurements of plate motion, confirming rates determined from magnetic anomaly patterns.

quiz_questions.question_text: Radiometric dating of basalt samples from the ocean floor shows that a sample collected 300 km from a ridge axis is 15 million years old. What is the half-spreading rate?
quiz_questions.explanation: Half-spreading rate = distance / time = 300 km / 15 million years = 20 km/Myr = 2 cm/year. Radiometric dating of ocean floor basalt (usually using potassium-argon or argon-argon methods) provides absolute ages that can be correlated with magnetic anomaly patterns.

quiz_questions.question_text: An echo sounder records a round-trip travel time of 6 seconds. If the speed of sound in seawater is 1,500 m/s, what is the ocean depth?
quiz_questions.explanation: Depth = (speed × time) / 2 = (1,500 m/s × 6 s) / 2 = 9,000 / 2 = 4,500 m. The division by 2 accounts for the fact that the sound must travel to the bottom and back, so the actual one-way distance is half the total path length.

quiz_questions.question_text: A seismic wave travels through the Earth at 8 km/s and takes 12 minutes to travel from an earthquake to a seismograph station. Approximately how far away is the earthquake?
quiz_questions.explanation: Distance = speed × time = 8 km/s × (12 min × 60 s/min) = 8 km/s × 720 s = 5,760 km. Seismic wave travel times are fundamental to locating earthquakes. Multiple seismograph stations are used to triangulate the exact location of an earthquake's epicenter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Heat flow measurements show that heat flow is highest at which ocean floor feature?
quiz_questions.explanation: Heat flow is highest at mid-ocean ridges because magma from the mantle is very close to the surface at these divergent boundaries. The hot mantle material that rises to create new crust conducts heat upward through the thin, young oceanic crust. Heat flow decreases with distance from the ridge as the crust ages and cools.

quiz_questions.question_text: If ocean crust at a distance of 100 km from a ridge axis has been dated to 4 million years old, and crust at 250 km is 10 million years old, is the spreading rate constant?

options.text: Yes, both give a rate of 2.5 cm/year
No, the rate has increased over time
No, the rate has decreased over time
Cannot be determined from this data

quiz_questions.explanation: Rate at 100 km: 100 km / 4 Myr = 25 km/Myr = 2.5 cm/yr. Rate at 250 km: 250 km / 10 Myr = 25 km/Myr = 2.5 cm/yr. Both rates are identical, indicating constant spreading over this time period. This consistency is typical of many ridge segments over millions of years.

quiz_questions.question_text: An isostasy calculation shows that a mountain made of crustal rock (density 2,700 kg/m³) floats on mantle rock (density 3,300 kg/m³). If the mountain rises 3 km above the surrounding surface, approximately how deep does its root extend below?
quiz_questions.explanation: Using isostasy: root depth = height × (crustal density) / (mantle density − crustal density) = 3 km × 2,700 / (3,300 − 2,700) = 3 × 2,700 / 600 = 3 × 4.5 = 13.5 km. This shows that mountains have deep roots extending into the mantle, like icebergs floating in water.

quiz_questions.question_text: A GPS station on a tectonic plate records 7 cm of northwestward movement per year. How many kilometers will the station move in 10 million years?
quiz_questions.explanation: Distance = rate × time = 7 cm/yr × 10,000,000 yr = 70,000,000 cm = 700,000 m = 700 km. GPS measurements of modern plate motion can be extrapolated to understand past and future plate positions, though rates may change over geological time.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'isostasy' in the context of plate tectonics?

options.text: The principle that Earth's crust floats in gravitational equilibrium on the denser mantle
The rate at which tectonic plates move across Earth's surface
The measurement of magnetic field strength in ocean rocks
The study of earthquake wave propagation

quiz_questions.explanation: Isostasy is the principle of buoyancy applied to Earth's crust: less dense crustal material floats on the denser underlying mantle, much like an iceberg floats in water. Thicker or less dense crust rises higher, forming mountains with deep roots, while thinner or denser oceanic crust sits lower.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'half-spreading rate'?

options.text: The average spreading rate of all mid-ocean ridges divided by two
The rate at which one plate moves away from the ridge axis
The rate of spreading only during the first half of the ridge's history
Half the rate of subduction at a convergent boundary

quiz_questions.explanation: The half-spreading rate measures the velocity of a single plate moving away from the ridge axis. The full spreading rate is the sum of both plates' half-rates, representing the total separation speed. If spreading is symmetric, the full rate is simply double the half-rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'radiometric dating'?

options.text: Dating rocks by measuring their distance from a mid-ocean ridge
Determining the age of rocks by measuring the decay of radioactive isotopes
Dating sediments by counting visible layers
Measuring the age of the ocean using its salt content

quiz_questions.explanation: Radiometric dating determines the age of rocks and minerals by measuring the ratio of a radioactive parent isotope to its stable daughter product. Common methods used for ocean floor basalt include potassium-argon (K-Ar) and argon-argon (⁴⁰Ar/³⁹Ar) dating, which can date rocks millions to billions of years old.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'magnetic anomaly' in marine geology?

options.text: An area where compass needles do not work
A variation in the magnetic field strength measured at the ocean surface, caused by normally or reversely magnetized crust below
A flaw in a magnetometer device
The magnetic field produced by ocean currents

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic anomaly is a local deviation from the expected magnetic field intensity, measured by ship-towed or airborne magnetometers. Positive anomalies occur over normally magnetized crust (reinforcing the current field), and negative anomalies occur over reversely magnetized crust (partially canceling the current field).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'P-wave' stand for in seismology?
quiz_questions.explanation: P-waves (primary waves) are compressional seismic waves that are the fastest type of seismic wave and the first to be recorded by seismographs after an earthquake. They travel through both solids and liquids by compressing and expanding material in the direction of wave travel.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'S-wave' in seismology?

options.text: A surface wave that travels only along Earth's exterior
A shear wave (secondary wave) that moves material perpendicular to its direction of travel
A sound wave used in echo sounding
A slow wave that only travels through water

quiz_questions.explanation: S-waves (secondary waves or shear waves) move material perpendicular to the direction of wave travel, creating a shearing motion. They are slower than P-waves and, critically, cannot travel through liquids. This property was used to determine that Earth's outer core is liquid.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'heat flow' measure in the context of the ocean floor?

options.text: The temperature of the ocean water at the surface
The rate at which heat is conducted from Earth's interior through the crust to the ocean floor surface
The heat produced by chemosynthetic bacteria at hydrothermal vents
The change in water temperature with depth in the ocean

quiz_questions.explanation: Heat flow is measured in milliwatts per square meter (mW/m²) and represents the rate at which heat from Earth's interior is conducted upward through the crust. High heat flow values at mid-ocean ridges indicate hot mantle material near the surface, while low values on old oceanic crust indicate cooled, thickened lithosphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'magnetometer'?

options.text: A device that measures gravitational pull
An instrument that measures the strength and direction of magnetic fields
A tool for dating rocks using radioactive decay
A device that measures ocean depth using sound

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetometer is an instrument used to measure the magnetic field intensity. In marine geology, ship-towed or airborne magnetometers detect magnetic anomalies in the oceanic crust, revealing the pattern of normal and reversed magnetic polarity that provides evidence for seafloor spreading.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'potassium-argon dating' determine?

options.text: The absolute age of volcanic rocks by measuring the ratio of potassium-40 to argon-40
The depth of the ocean floor

quiz_questions.explanation: Potassium-argon (K-Ar) dating measures the ratio of radioactive potassium-40 to its decay product argon-40 in volcanic rocks. Since the half-life of K-40 is 1.25 billion years, this method is well-suited for dating ocean floor basalts that are millions to billions of years old.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'plate velocity' in tectonic studies?

options.text: The speed and direction at which a tectonic plate moves across Earth's surface
The speed at which seismic waves travel through a plate
The rate at which a plate sinks during subduction
The speed of volcanic eruptions at plate boundaries

quiz_questions.explanation: Plate velocity is a vector quantity that describes both the speed and direction at which a tectonic plate moves. It can be measured using GPS stations, calculated from magnetic anomaly patterns, or inferred from hotspot tracks. Typical plate velocities range from 1 to 16 cm/year.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists discover that magnetic stripe A on the east side of a ridge is 75 km from the axis and 3 million years old, while the corresponding stripe A on the west side is 100 km from the axis and also 3 million years old. What can be concluded?

options.text: The ridge is spreading symmetrically at 5.8 cm/year
Spreading is asymmetric: the west plate moves faster (3.3 cm/yr) than the east plate (2.5 cm/yr)
The data is invalid because spreading must always be symmetric

quiz_questions.explanation: East half-rate = 75 km / 3 Myr = 25 km/Myr = 2.5 cm/yr. West half-rate = 100 km / 3 Myr = 33.3 km/Myr = 3.3 cm/yr. The west plate is moving faster, showing asymmetric spreading. The full spreading rate is 2.5 + 3.3 = 5.8 cm/yr. Asymmetric spreading is common at many ridges.

quiz_questions.question_text: A basalt sample from the ocean floor is dated using potassium-argon dating. The sample contains 75% of its original potassium-40. If the half-life of K-40 is 1.25 billion years, approximately how old is the sample?
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the decay equation: fraction remaining = (1/2)^(t/half-life). If 75% remains: 0.75 = (0.5)^(t/1.25 Gyr). Taking the log: ln(0.75) = (t/1.25) × ln(0.5). Solving: t = 1.25 × ln(0.75)/ln(0.5) = 1.25 × (−0.2877)/(−0.6931) ≈ 1.25 × 0.415 ≈ 0.52 billion years = ~520 million years.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher measures heat flow at three locations along a transect from a mid-ocean ridge: 200 mW/m² at the ridge crest, 80 mW/m² at 50 km distance, and 50 mW/m² at 200 km distance. What does this pattern indicate?

options.text: The mantle beneath the ridge is cooling from east to west
Heat flow decreases with distance from the ridge because the crust cools and thickens as it moves away
Higher heat flow at the ridge is caused by frictional heating between the plates
Sediment thickness increases heat flow near the ridge

quiz_questions.explanation: The decrease in heat flow with distance from the ridge reflects the cooling and thickening of the lithosphere as new crust moves away from the heat source at the ridge. Near the ridge, hot mantle material is close to the surface. As the plate moves away, it cools, the lithosphere thickens, and less heat reaches the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two GPS stations are located 4,000 km apart across an ocean. Measurements show they are converging at 8 cm/year. If subduction began at the trench between them 50 million years ago, how much oceanic plate has been subducted?
quiz_questions.explanation: Amount subducted = rate × time = 8 cm/yr × 50,000,000 yr = 400,000,000 cm = 4,000 km. This means 4,000 km of oceanic plate has been consumed at the subduction zone. This is a simplified calculation that assumes a constant convergence rate over 50 million years.

quiz_questions.question_text: The ocean floor at point X is 120 km from a ridge axis and 8 million years old. The ocean floor at point Y is 300 km from the same ridge axis and 15 million years old. Both are on the same side. Has the half-spreading rate been constant?

options.text: Yes, both give the same half-spreading rate
No, the rate was slower earlier (1.5 cm/yr for point X) and faster now (2 cm/yr for point Y)
No, the rate was faster earlier (2 cm/yr at point Y) and decreased to 1.5 cm/yr at point X
Cannot be determined without additional data

quiz_questions.explanation: Rate at X (younger, closer): 120 km / 8 Myr = 15 km/Myr = 1.5 cm/yr. Rate at Y (older, farther): 300 km / 15 Myr = 20 km/Myr = 2 cm/yr. Point Y's rate represents an average over the older period, and point X's rate represents a more recent average. The overall rate has decreased from ~2 cm/yr to ~1.5 cm/yr.

quiz_questions.question_text: A continental block of average density 2,700 kg/m³ has a total thickness of 35 km and floats on mantle rock of density 3,300 kg/m³. Using isostasy, what fraction of the block extends below the surface of the mantle?
quiz_questions.explanation: For isostatic equilibrium: depth submerged / total thickness = crustal density / mantle density = 2,700 / 3,300 ≈ 0.818 or about 82%. This means approximately 82% of the continental block sits below the mantle surface, analogous to how about 90% of an iceberg sits below the water surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists use three seismograph stations to locate an earthquake. The P-wave arrives at station A 4 minutes before the S-wave, at station B 6 minutes before the S-wave, and at station C 8 minutes before the S-wave. Which station is closest to the earthquake?
quiz_questions.explanation: The time difference between P-wave and S-wave arrivals increases with distance from the earthquake source, because P-waves travel faster than S-waves and the gap between them grows over greater distances. Station A has the smallest P-S time difference (4 minutes), indicating it is closest to the epicenter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mid-ocean ridge segment has a full spreading rate of 10 cm/year. At this rate, how long would it take to create 500 km of new ocean floor (measuring from ridge axis to one side)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Since the full spreading rate is 10 cm/yr, the half-spreading rate (rate at which crust moves from the axis to one side) is 5 cm/yr. Time = distance / rate = 500 km / (5 cm/yr) = 500,000,000 cm / 5 cm/yr = 10,000,000 years = 10 million years.

quiz_questions.question_text: Oceanic crust has an average density of 3,000 kg/m³ and a thickness of 7 km. Continental crust has an average density of 2,700 kg/m³ and a thickness of 35 km. Both float on mantle rock at 3,300 kg/m³. Using isostasy, which crustal type sits higher above the mantle surface, and why?

options.text: Oceanic crust sits higher because it is thinner
Both sit at the same height because isostasy balances them equally
Continental crust sits higher because it is thicker and less dense, displacing more mantle
Oceanic crust sits higher because it is denser and more buoyant

quiz_questions.explanation: Height above mantle = total thickness − submerged depth. Oceanic: submerged = 7 × (3,000/3,300) = 6.36 km; height above = 7 − 6.36 = 0.64 km. Continental: submerged = 35 × (2,700/3,300) = 28.6 km; height above = 35 − 28.6 = 6.4 km. Continental crust sits ~6.4 km above the mantle surface versus ~0.64 km for oceanic crust.

quiz_questions.question_text: A magnetic survey reveals five symmetric pairs of magnetic stripes on each side of a ridge. The outermost stripe boundary is 180 km from the ridge axis and corresponds to a known magnetic reversal that occurred 9 million years ago. A second stripe boundary at 80 km corresponds to a reversal 4 million years ago. Has the spreading rate been constant? What are the rates?

options.text: Yes; constant at 2 cm/year on each side
No; the rate was faster in the past (3 cm/yr) and slower now (1 cm/yr)
No; the rate has increased from 1 cm/yr to 3 cm/yr
Cannot be determined from only two data points

quiz_questions.explanation: Outer stripe: half-rate = 180 km / 9 Myr = 20 km/Myr = 2 cm/yr. Inner stripe: half-rate = 80 km / 4 Myr = 20 km/Myr = 2 cm/yr. Both rates are identical at 2 cm/year, indicating a constant half-spreading rate. The full spreading rate is 4 cm/year. Consistent rates across multiple stripe boundaries strengthen this conclusion.
